abstract: This article explores the actual role and character of the Pattani Central Mosque, offering a counter-narrative to the common depiction of mosques in Southern Thailand as mere breeding grounds for Islamic radicalism and the recruitment of "martyrs." In the wake of the 2004 Kru-ze Mosque incident, which starkly demonstrated the failure of sacred spaces to serve as sanctuaries from violence, serious questions have emerged regarding the nature of religious life in the region. By examining Islamic teaching practices, mosque management, and the perspectives of key stakeholders—including Imams, preachers, and the congregation—this study evaluates local attitudes toward good governance, human rights, and the prevention of extremism. Contrary to alarmist portrayals, the findings suggest that the Pattani Central Mosque functions as a space primarily defined by a balanced blend of moderate and conservative believers, showing little inclination toward the radical or extreme tendencies often cited in political discourse.
